design_philosophy.core_loop: Select fighter → fight sequence of opponents → manage health across multiple bouts → defeat final boss (Masked Warrior)
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Depleting opponent health before own health depletes; managing stamina across 15 sequential fights with no breaks between rounds
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Underground pit fighter rising through ranks to challenge the mysterious Masked Warrior; arcade brawler with minimal rules
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Digitized live-action sprites instead of drawn animation; early technical showcase prioritizing visual novelty over mechanical refinement; accessibility-first fighting game with simple button inputs (punch/kick/super move via all three buttons); multiplayer integration with AI clones in Grudge Matches

difficulty.continues_system: Grudge Match elimination system: getting knocked down three times in a Grudge Match eliminates player, but losing Grudge Match doesn't eliminate player from campaign; bonus money awarded to winner
difficulty.checkpointing: 15 sequential fights with no mid-campaign saves; Grudge Matches occur every third fight as checkpoint-like breather rounds
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Character selection offers different speed/power tradeoffs (Buzz strong but slow, Ty agile, Kato quick with martial arts); three-button super move mechanic provides tactical depth; power pill pickups grant temporary invincibility frames

progression.upgrade_system: Character stat differences (speed, power, move sets); power pill consumables during fights
progression.unlock_mechanism: Sequential fight progression unlocks next opponent; Masked Warrior finale unlocked after defeating 15 primary opponents
replayability_hooks: Three distinct playable characters with different move sets and stats encourage replay
Multiplayer modes support up to three simultaneous players with dynamic AI substitution
Grudge Match multiplayer battles create emergent competitive scenarios
Different fight outcomes affect bonus money rewards

world_building_techniques: Mysterious Masked Warrior taunts between matches, creating narrative intrigue
Character archetypes (ex-wrestler Buzz, kickboxing champion Ty, martial artist Kato) establish fighter identities
Opponent roster includes distinct personas (Executioner, Southside Jim, Heavy Metal, Chainman Eddie) suggesting underground fighting culture
Digitized live actors ground narrative in pseudo-realism versus fantasy sprites

boss_design.philosophy: Final challenge against singular mysterious opponent (Masked Warrior) after tournament progression; represents culmination rather than mechanical pivot
notable_bosses: Masked Warrior (final opponent, Bill McAleenan) - taunts between matches, fight mechanics consistent with other opponents rather than distinct phase patterns

faction_indicators: Three playable character archetypes suggest ethnic/professional diversity (All-American wrestler, African-American kickboxer, Japanese-American martial artist)
Opponent roster reflects underground fighting culture demographics
danger_signals: SNES version removes three characters and interactive crowd mechanics versus arcade original (content cuts suggest platform limitations or design choices)
Unused graphics ('TIME UP', 'ELIMINATION') indicate planned mechanics removed during development
environmental_storytelling: Crowd interaction (attacking, weapon drops, pushing fighters) creates living fight environment
Pit setting implied by protagonist identities and opponent roster
Bonus rounds and Grudge Match mechanics suggest escalating stakes narrative
